Hey everyone, Sam Evans here and welcome to this training module called Websites and DM retainers, which means digital marketing retainers. So in this training module here, I'm going to give you the full overview of what these actually are, like what are websites and how do we package them, price them and sell them, and also what are DM retainers, how do we package them, price them and sell them, and how do we deliver the services to our clients too, like what sort of contractors do we need, what do they need to be good at, and all of that stuff. So in this video, we're really going to dive into the things we're offering, and I'm going to break them apart and show you everything about them so that you can fully understand them. So here's what we're going to be covering on today's video. What websites and DM retainers are and how they work, and then we're going to go into each one specifically. So we'll start with websites, how we package, price and position websites, then how we package, price and position DM retainers, and then what's involved in the service delivery of websites and then what's involved in the service delivery of DM retainers. So we're going to go through all of this stuff here. So what websites and DM retainers are and how they work. So let's start with websites. So a website is a collection of pages hosted on the internet that's successful via a domain name like SamOvans.com or Consulting.com. I'm pretty sure everyone understands what a website is, so we don't know to spend too much time going over that. So business owners want websites for two key reasons. Number one is they want to look good, and they have emotional attachment or ego attached to it, and that's a huge reason why people buy websites and want website upgrades. And the second one is they want more customers and they think a better website will do that for them. So those are the two reasons why people buy them. And these are one-off sales that typically range from $3,000 to $10,000 each. So at the lower end, you're looking about 3K, high end, 10K. So what's involved when building a website? Well we build a website using a platform such as WordPress, and then we assist in the design, the site structure, the content, and the layout, etc. Now let's talk about digital marketing retainers. What are they and how do they work? So DM retainer is a term that we use in this program, and the public has no idea what it is. Like, so don't be like, when you're talking to clients, don't refer to them as DM retainers or anything like that because people won't know what you're talking about. We just use it as a term in this program and that's really the only place you'll hear about it. So DM stands for digital marketing and a retainer is a fee that somebody pays monthly for an ongoing business relationship. So in our DM retainers, we sell either Google AdWords, set up in management, or Facebook Ads, set up in management. And we don't provide both because that's too complex. So we choose the one that works best for our specific niche, which might be something like dentists, plumbers, yoga instructors, e-commerce businesses, whatever. And these are ongoing recurring sales that range from one grand a month to five grand a month and it builds every month. So it's recurring revenue, unlike websites which are typically sold as a one-off sale. So what's involved with DM retainers? Well, we construct a strategy and then we build landing pages, create advertising campaigns, and then improve it in management on an ongoing basis. So a lot of people think with DM retainers, they're like, why would somebody pay me month after month for them? And the reason why is because nothing in business is really set and forget. Like you can't set up a Facebook campaign or a Google AdWords campaign and then leave it and it's just going to work. That does not happen. There is not an instance of that anywhere in the world. Once you've set something up and got it to work, that's just the half of it. Then you've got to maintain it on an ongoing basis and that's why there's recurring revenue. With a website, we don't really need to maintain it on an ongoing basis. So it's mostly just set up and then they might wait a year or two years and then they decide they want to refresh, then it's another project. So it's dealt with on a one-off basis. So now let's talk about how we package, price, and sell both websites and DM retainers. Let's start with websites. How we package, price, and position them. So with websites, you want to sell them a complete, done-for-you package at a specific price and you always want to provide two options. There's a lot of psychology in providing two options to people because they feel like they've got a choice and when there's a higher end option and a lower end option, it makes the lower end option seem a lot cheaper. Whereas if we just presented them with a lower end option, they might think that's expensive. So it's very powerful putting two things together and I recommend you always do it. So the lower end option, full website, this is what's included in it. Full website design and development using WordPress, assistance on copywriting and the use of images, assistance in making sure the website is structured well for converting visitors into customers. And we charge a total price for these of $5,000. That's the lower end option. The cheapest you'd ever want to sell a website for would probably be $3,000, but I recommend starting selling websites at $5,000. And then the higher end option. So this one includes full website design and development using WordPress, full copywriting service, use of email capture forms and autoresponder integration, and conversion rate optimization and SEO best practices. Total price $10,000. So the difference between the lower and the higher end option is essentially we include email capture forms and autoresponders and we also make sure that we're following SEO best practices. So we make sure that we set the meta titles, the meta tags, the meta descriptions. We make sure that we've got all of that stuff set up correctly and optimized to rank in the search engines. We also want to make sure the site structure and everything is done well for search engines. And we also include the autoresponders and that sort of stuff, which is, you know, it makes it a better website and a higher end website. And this gives the client a choice between a five-gram one or a 10-gram one. And that's what I recommend offering. The lowest start offer your low end one would be three and the lowest start offer your high end one would be, say, six. So if you're really nervous going into it, then you can price the lower end one at $3,000, higher end one at $6,000. Now let's talk about DM retainers, how we package, price and position them. So just like we do with websites, you want to provide people with two options, a basic and a pro. So we've got AdWords Basic and then AdWords Pro. So with AdWords Basic, we provide them with market research to figure out what's working in the market, like what their competitors are doing, what's the best practice. Then planning and strategy, so we want to devise a plan, map it all out. Then we want to create the landing pages for them. Then we want to set up their campaign and then we want to test it to make sure it's working, go live and then provide ongoing management. And the price for that is $2,000 per month recurring. And then AdWords Pro. And this is extensive market research, which means we just go a little bit above and beyond what we would do in AdWords Basic. We do deeper market research, deeper planning and strategy. And then instead of just creating landing pages, we might create two landing page options for each landing page and then split test it to make sure that we've got an optimized one. And then we would do the campaign setup and then some vigorous conversion rate optimization and ongoing management. And we would price that at $3,500 a month recurring. So those would be the two options for DM retainers for AdWords. Now let's talk about Facebook. Facebook Basic and Facebook Pro. So with Facebook Basic, we provide market research, planning and strategy, landing page creation, campaign setup and then testing and ongoing management. And the total price for this would be $2,000 a month recurring. And then with Facebook Pro, we do extensive market research, planning and strategy, split tested landing pages and campaign setup, multi-channel retargeting and vigorous conversion rate optimization and ongoing management. So the differences between Basic and Pro with Facebook is we create an AB version for each landing page to test it. And then when it comes to retargeting, we might set up retargeting with a perfect audience so that we can get some banners out there too. And then we just pay more attention to the conversion rate optimization. And the price for that is $3,500 a month. So that's how I would offer my services if I was offering AdWords or Facebook to my particular niche. Now let's talk about how we deliver websites and DM retainers as a service to clients. So if you went out and you sold a new website client, you'd start off by doing market research to find out the best practices in the industry. And what I mean by this is, you know, whenever you're doing a project for a client and whenever you're doing something for yourself, the best thing to do is just find out what other people are doing. It's the best place to start. And you want to copy them or mimic them. And I don't mean like copy, paste their stuff. I mean just copy the fundamentals of it. Because people have always figured out what works in particular industries and the best starting point is to copy them. So let's say that you're doing plumbers, for example. So you'd want to find out who the best plumbing businesses are in this particular area. So let's say we're in Auckland, New Zealand and we're doing a job for a plumber in Auckland, New Zealand. And I would ask him, I'd say, hey, who are the people that you're aware of in your industry who are just crushing it? Who are the market leaders? And I'd write down the names of them. And this works with any industry. Anyone you ask is always going to know who the leaders of their market are. And the best thing to do is ask the client, who are the market leaders, write them down, go and research the market leaders, go and see what they're doing, what do their websites look like. And then once you've found that out, then you want to create a wireframe for the website. And you can do this just in keynote by dragging things around. And then they're talking about the different problems. Again, stating the phone number, what the price is, and then saying what they do. All right, now the next step would be to go to themeforest.com and find a theme there. And the best way to do it is to start by just typing in like plumbing in WordPress. So you want to look at some of these and find ones that you think look good. And let's say we think this one's a good option and these other two that we found are good options. So what you want to do is you've done the market research, then you've created the wireframe, and then you want to come up with three themes from themeforest. You want to find the three themes. And then what you want to do is you want to send an email to your client and say, hey, we're just trying to decide on the theme and the look and the style for this website. And I've found these three themes, which seem to be working the best for plumbers right now, out of these three themes, which one do you like the most? And they'll tell you. They'll say this one. I like this one. You know, everyone's able to choose out of three things. What I find is a bad thing to do is to go to the client and be like, what do you want your website to look like? They won't know. It's best to put some options in front of someone and get them to choose from that. It makes it way easier. So by doing our market research and then going to theme forest and finding three which we think are good, then getting the client to choose one of those three, that's the easiest way to do it. You want to do a thorough check over yourself first. Don't just send it to your client first. You should really do the first review. So you're reviewing it yourself and you're looking for any errors or anything like that. And then if you find anything, tell the developer or the contractor to fix it, get it fixed, check it on mobile, check it on desktop, check over everything, make sure it looks good. Once you've done that, now you can send version one over to your client to get feedback. Now I recommend that you only do three versions and three rounds of reviews. Because what can happen when you're selling websites to people is you'll just end up in an endless cycle, an endless loop of them asking for more changes, more changes, more changes, more changes, and the job will never get finished. So up front you want to tell people that you only offer three rounds of reviews. And this first round of reviews is happening right now. And now all you want to do is move what's on the test server over to the live server so it's on their main domain name. Now to do this, you just want to ask the client when they would like to do it. Sometimes it's a good idea to do the migration like at nighttime because they might be busy during the day and sometimes the website can go down for like half an hour. Like usually it doesn't go down at all, but you want to be cautious. Sometimes it can go down for half an hour to an hour. So that's why it's best to do it at nighttime. For some businesses, they might only want to do it on the weekends. That's why you want to schedule a date with your client. One's going to be cold. We were targeting total strangers and trying to get them to come over to our site. And the other one might be warmer targeting. And this one is just targeting people who have visited our website in the past, say, 30, 60, 90 days. And then designing the KPIs for the campaign. So we want to figure out what is our main KPI. Now let's say we're doing plumbers, it might be getting them customers or it might be getting them phone calls or inquiries. So let's say we figure out that if we get them five inquiries, then for every five inquiries they get, they get a customer. And if they get a customer, on average, that customer is worth $1,000. I'm making these numbers up, but this is what you have to talk to your clients about and understand. So if we get them a client for $1,000 and it takes five inquiries to do that, and the max they're willing to pay to get a $1,000 client is, say, $200. Or then $200 divided by five, there's about $40 per inquiry. So really we're trying to use Facebook ads to get inquiries for less than $40 each. If we can do that, then all the numbers work together. And that's how we design out the KPIs. And to design the KPIs, you want to talk to your client and see what's the average customer worth to them. How much are they able to pay to acquire a customer and still make a profit? How many inquiries do they usually need to get to convert one of those into a customer? And still to this day, I don't really know how to do it. And then websites, I still don't know how to code. I've always used contractors to do that. The only skill I really mastered for myself was Facebook because I wanted to learn how to do it for myself. But back in the day when I was selling to my initial clients, I used contractors for everything. Facebook, AdWords and the websites, I didn't do any of the work myself. So that is how it works. So that's it for this training video. Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next one.
